## 4080environmental-project.md
# Project: PXIe-4080 Gain Error Analysis (4080Environmental)

## Location
- Project root: C:\Users\ghargrea\Documents\Github\01 Jpy Notebooks\4080Environmental
- Git-tracked (synced across machines via GitHub).
- Files: tdms_gain_error_plot.ipynb (primary), tdms_gain_error_plot.py (original script), requirements.txt, .gitignore, .venv/ (ignored)

## Purpose
Parse NI PXIe-4080 TDMS files and plot gain error for 10V and 300V ranges across all DUTs, with chamber temp & humidity overlays.

## Environment
- venv at .venv (uv-managed base interpreter: cpython-3.14.4 in uv path; do NOT pip-install into the base, it's externally managed - use `uv venv` + `uv pip`).
- Jupyter kernel registered: name "4080environmental", display "Python (4080Environmental)". Notebook kernelspec bound to it.
- Recreate env on new machine: `uv venv .venv` ; `uv pip install -r requirements.txt` ; then `python -m ipykernel install --user --name 4080environmental --display-name "Python (4080Environmental)"`
- Deps: nptdms, pandas, numpy, scikit-learn, matplotlib, ipykernel, ipympl==0.10.0
- Notebook uses `%matplotlib widget` (ipympl) in imports cell for interactive zoom/pan/scroll plots. Switch to `%matplotlib inline` for static. .py script uses tkagg GUI window.

## Data
- ROOT_PATH currently C:\temp\EnvironmentalTesting\Round2Data (NOT in repo). One subfolder per DUT serial; .tdms files inside.
- OPEN QUESTION: where TDMS data should live long-term (repo is risky if large). ROOT_PATH may need per-machine config.

## TDMS channel names (group = first group, "Measured Data")
- 300V: "Test Points 300V", "DUT Measurements 300V" (fallback to "Test Points"/"DUT Measurements")
- 10V: "Test Points 10V", "DUT Measurements 10V"
- Chamber: "Chamber Temperature 300V" (fallback "Chamber Temperature"), "Chamber RH% 300V" (fallback "Chamber RH%")
- Also present: "Error VDC 300V/10V", "SN 300V/10V" (not used for gain calc)

## Logic / decisions
- Gain error (ppm) = (slope - 1) * 1e6 from LinearRegression fit of DUT Measurements vs Test Points. Done per range.
- Filename parsing: serial_Temp_Set_<t>_RH_Set_<rh>_M_D_Y_H_M_S_AM/PM. Serials are HEX strings.
- Serial ordering: ascending by hex value (serial_sort_key). Per serial plot 10V first then 300V.
- Colors: one unique color per serial shared by its 10V (dashed, square 's') and 300V (solid, round 'o') traces. tab10 if <=10 serials else tab20.
- Outlier filter: omit any point where abs(gain_error) > 100 ppm (GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM in notebook).
- Chamber temp/RH plotted on dedup-by-timestamp env_df to avoid double-counting per-range rows. ax2=temp(red), ax3=RH(green, spine outward 60).
- Output saved to gain_error_plot.png.

## Run command (script)
- `.\.venv\Scripts\python.exe .\tdms_gain_error_plot.py` from project root.

toolCallRounds.response:
Confirmed intact — that file is your full transcript from June 11 in VS Code's standard chat format (1.5 MB). Here's what's actually going on:

## Your chat history is safe — nothing is lost

Your real conversation transcripts are on disk and intact:

| Session | File | Size | Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| June 11 conversation | `chatSessions/1d408dcf….jsonl` | **1.5 MB** | 6/11 |
| Today's conversation | `chatSessions/773736af….jsonl` | 24 KB | 6/12 |

These are the files VS Code reads to show the chat history list (the clock/"Show Chats" icon in the Chat view). The data is all there.

## What *is* empty: the Chronicle session index

The thing reporting "no sessions" is the **Chronicle session store** — the SQLite database that the session-history query tool reads. It is built from a *separate* set of logs:

- `…/GitHub.copilot-chat/debug-logs/<id>/main.jsonl`

Those files are only **264 bytes** each and contain a single line — a `session_start` event — with **no conversation turns**:

```json
{"v":1,"type":"session_start","sid":"1d408dcf…","attrs":{"copilotVersion":"0.48.1","vscodeVersion":"1.119.0"}}
```

That's why a reindex found 2 sessions but **0 turns / 0 files / 0 summaries**. Chronicle has nothing to index because the per-turn debug logging isn't writing anything beyond the start marker.

## Why the turns aren't being logged

Chronicle's `main.jsonl` only records full turn/tool data when debug session logging is actively writing. The fact that both sessions (yesterday and today) show only a `session_start` line means turn-level logging is effectively disabled or not flushing — it's a logging/config gap, **not** data loss. The two systems are independent: your chat panel history comes from `chatSessions/`, while `/chronicle` queries come from `debug-logs/`.

Let me confirm whether a setting is suppressing the turn logging.

## Summary

**Your chat history is not gone.** Two complete transcripts are saved on disk for this workspace:

edits.text:
<#
.SYNOPSIS
    Exports VS Code Copilot chat history for THIS project into the repo so it
    syncs to GitHub alongside the code.

.DESCRIPTION
    VS Code stores chat transcripts outside the project, in your user profile:
        %APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<hash>\
    The <hash> is derived per-machine/per-path, so this script does NOT hardcode
    it. Instead it scans every workspaceStorage folder, reads its workspace.json,
    and matches the one whose "folder" URI points at this repo. It then copies:
        - chatSessions\*.jsonl        (the full chat transcripts)
        - chatEditingSessions\*       (edit/checkpoint state)
        - GitHub.copilot-chat\debug-logs\*  (Chronicle session logs)
    into chat-history\ under the repo, and writes a manifest.

    Run it whenever you want a snapshot (e.g. before `git push`). Existing files
    are overwritten with the latest versions; nothing in your profile is changed.

.NOTES
    Safe & read-only with respect to VS Code's own storage (copy only).
#>

[CmdletBinding()]
param(
    # Set of VS Code variants to search (stable + insiders).
    [string[]] $CodeVariants = @('Code', 'Code - Insiders')
)

$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'

# --- Resolve paths -----------------------------------------------------------
# This script lives in <repo>\chat-history\, so the repo root is its parent.
$ScriptDir = Split-Path -Parent $MyInvocation.MyCommand.Path
$RepoRoot  = Split-Path -Parent $ScriptDir
$DestRoot  = $ScriptDir   # export into chat-history\ itself

Write-Host "Repo root      : $RepoRoot"
Write-Host "Export target  : $DestRoot"

# Normalize the repo root to a comparable form (lowercased full path).
$RepoRootNorm = ([System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($RepoRoot)).TrimEnd('\').ToLowerInvariant()

# --- Helper: decode a file:// URI to a Windows path --------------------------
function ConvertFrom-FileUri {
    param([string] $Uri)
    if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($Uri)) { return $null }
    try {
        $u = [System.Uri]$Uri
        if ($u.Scheme -ne 'file') { return $null }
        # LocalPath handles %20 etc. and gives a Windows path.
        return ([System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($u.LocalPath)).TrimEnd('\').ToLowerInvariant()
    } catch {
        return $null
    }
}

# --- Locate the matching workspaceStorage folder ----------------------------
$appData = $env:APPDATA
if (-not $appData) { throw "APPDATA environment variable is not set." }

$matched = @()
foreach ($variant in $CodeVariants) {
    $wsStorage = Join-Path $appData "$variant\User\workspaceStorage"
    if (-not (Test-Path $wsStorage)) { continue }

    Get-ChildItem $wsStorage -Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | ForEach-Object {
        $wsJson = Join-Path $_.FullName 'workspace.json'
        if (Test-Path $wsJson) {
            try {
                $folderUri = (Get-Content $wsJson -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json).folder
                $folderPath = ConvertFrom-FileUri $folderUri
                if ($folderPath -and $folderPath -eq $RepoRootNorm) {
                    $matched += [pscustomobject]@{
                        Variant = $variant
                        Hash    = $_.Name
                        Storage = $_.FullName
                    }
                }
            } catch { }
        }
    }
}

if ($matched.Count -eq 0) {
    Write-Warning "No VS Code workspaceStorage folder matched this repo path:"
    Write-Warning "  $RepoRootNorm"
    Write-Warning "Open this folder in VS Code at least once, then re-run."
    exit 1
}

Write-Host "Matched $($matched.Count) workspace storage folder(s)."

# --- Copy the chat data ------------------------------------------------------
$manifest = [System.Collections.Generic.List[object]]::new()

foreach ($m in $matched) {
    Write-Host "`n--- $($m.Variant)  [$($m.Hash)] ---"
    $destBase = Join-Path $DestRoot $m.Hash
    New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $destBase | Out-Null

    $sources = @(
        @{ Name = 'chatSessions';        Path = Join-Path $m.Storage 'chatSessions' },
        @{ Name = 'chatEditingSessions'; Path = Join-Path $m.Storage 'chatEditingSessions' },
        @{ Name = 'debug-logs';          Path = Join-Path $m.Storage 'GitHub.copilot-chat\debug-logs' }
    )

    foreach ($src in $sources) {
        if (Test-Path $src.Path) {
            $destSub = Join-Path $destBase $src.Name
            New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $destSub | Out-Null
            Copy-Item -Path (Join-Path $src.Path '*') -Destination $destSub -Recurse -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
            $count = (Get-ChildItem $destSub -Recurse -File -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Measure-Object).Count
            Write-Host ("  {0,-20} {1,4} file(s)" -f $src.Name, $count)
            $manifest.Add([pscustomobject]@{
                variant   = $m.Variant
                hash      = $m.Hash
                category  = $src.Name
                fileCount = $count
            })
        } else {
            Write-Host ("  {0,-20} (none)" -f $src.Name)
        }
    }
}

# --- Write manifest ----------------------------------------------------------
$manifestPath = Join-Path $DestRoot 'last-export.json'
[pscustomobject]@{
    exportedAt = (Get-Date).ToString('o')
    repoRoot   = $RepoRoot
    machine    = $env:COMPUTERNAME
    user       = $env:USERNAME
    entries    = $manifest
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5 | Set-Content -Path $manifestPath -Encoding UTF8

Write-Host "`nExport complete. Manifest: $manifestPath"
Write-Host "Now commit & push:  git add chat-history; git commit -m 'Snapshot chat history'; git push"

edits.text:
# Chat History (Copilot session backups)

This folder preserves the VS Code / GitHub Copilot **chat transcripts** for this
project so they are committed to Git and survive across machines and reinstalls.

## Why this exists

VS Code stores chat history **outside** the project, in your user profile:

```
%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<hash>\chatSessions\*.jsonl
```

That folder is **not** part of the repo, so a normal `git push` does *not* back
up your conversations. If the profile is wiped, the history is lost even though
the code is safe on GitHub. This folder fixes that.

## How to use

Run the export script whenever you want to snapshot the latest chat history
(e.g. before pushing):

```powershell
.\chat-history\export-chat-history.ps1
git add chat-history
git commit -m "Snapshot chat history"
git push
```

The script auto-detects the correct `workspaceStorage` folder by matching this
repo's path (the `<hash>` differs per machine, so it is not hardcoded). It copies:

| Category              | What it is                                  |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| `chatSessions`        | Full chat transcripts (`*.jsonl`)           |
| `chatEditingSessions` | Edit / checkpoint state                     |
| `debug-logs`          | Chronicle session logs                      |

Each machine's data is stored under its own `<hash>/` subfolder, and
`last-export.json` records the most recent export.

## Restoring on a new machine

These files are a **backup/record**. To view a transcript, open the `.jsonl`
under `<hash>/chatSessions/`. To make VS Code itself show them in the Chat
history list, copy the contents of `<hash>/chatSessions/` back into that
machine's `%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<new-hash>\chatSessions\`
(find `<new-hash>` via the `workspace.json` whose `folder` points at this repo).

# VS Code writes URIs like  file:///c%3A/Users/.../Project  where the drive
# colon is percent-encoded. System.Uri.LocalPath mishandles that, so decode
# manually: strip the scheme, URL-unescape (%3A -> :, %20 -> space), then
# normalize slashes and any leading slash before the drive letter.
function ConvertFrom-FileUri {
    param([string] $Uri)
    if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($Uri)) { return $null }
    try {
        $p = $Uri -replace '^file:/+', ''          # drop "file:///" (any # of slashes)
        $p = [System.Uri]::UnescapeDataString($p)  # %3A -> :  ,  %20 -> space
        $p = $p -replace '/', '\'                   # forward -> back slashes
        $p = $p -replace '^\\+', ''                 # drop any leading backslash
        return ([System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($p

toolCalls.arguments:
{"content": "# Chat History (Copilot session backups)

This folder preserves the VS Code / GitHub Copilot **chat transcripts** for this
project so they are committed to Git and survive across machines and reinstalls.

## Why this exists

VS Code stores chat history **outside** the project, in your user profile:

```
%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<hash>\chatSessions\*.jsonl
```

That folder is **not** part of the repo, so a normal `git push` does *not* back
up your conversations. If the profile is wiped, the history is lost even though
the code is safe on GitHub. This folder fixes that.

## How to use

Run the export script whenever you want to snapshot the latest chat history
(e.g. before pushing):

```powershell
.\chat-history\export-chat-history.ps1
git add chat-history
git commit -m "Snapshot chat history"
git push
```

The script auto-detects the correct `workspaceStorage` folder by matching this
repo's path (the `<hash>` differs per machine, so it is not hardcoded). It copies:

| Category              | What it is                                  |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| `chatSessions`        | Full chat transcripts (`*.jsonl`)           |
| `chatEditingSessions` | Edit / checkpoint state                     |
| `debug-logs`          | Chronicle session logs                      |

Each machine's data is stored under its own `<hash>/` subfolder, and
`last-export.json` records the most recent export.

## Restoring on a new machine

These files are a **backup/record**. To view a transcript, open the `.jsonl`
under `<hash>/chatSessions/`. To make VS Code itself show them in the Chat
history list, copy the contents of `<hash>/chatSessions/` back into that
machine's `%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<new-hash>\chatSessions\`
(find `<new-hash>` via the `workspace.json` whose `folder` points at this repo).

edits.text:
#!/bin/sh
# Pre-commit hook: snapshot Copilot chat history into the repo so it is
# committed alongside the code. Runs the PowerShell export script, then stages
# the chat-history folder so it is part of THIS commit.
#
# Enable on a new machine with:
#   git config core.hooksPath .githooks
#
# To skip a single commit:  git commit --no-verify

repo_root=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
script="$repo_root/chat-history/export-chat-history.ps1"

if [ ! -f "$script" ]; then
    echo "pre-commit: chat-history export script not found, skipping."
    exit 0
fi

# Find a PowerShell executable (Windows PowerShell or PowerShell Core).
ps_exe=""
for c in powershell.exe pwsh.exe pwsh; do
    if command -v "$c" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        ps_exe="$c"
        break
    fi
done

if [ -z "$ps_exe" ]; then
    echo "pre-commit: PowerShell not found on PATH, skipping chat-history export."
    exit 0
fi

echo "pre-commit: exporting Copilot chat history..."
"$ps_exe" -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "$script"

# Stage whatever the export produced/updated.
git add "$repo_root/chat-history"

exit 0

Short answer: **the interactive (zoomable/pannable) plot will NOT be saved** in a usable way. You'll need to rerun to get the interactive version back. Here's the detail:

## Why

Your imports cell uses `%matplotlib widget` (the ipympl backend). That interactive canvas — the one you zoom and pan — is a **live widget backed by the running kernel**. It only works while the kernel is alive and connected. When you:

- **Save the notebook** → the interactive widget state is generally *not* persisted as reusable output. Even when a placeholder is stored, reopening shows a dead/blank widget or a "Loading widget…" message.
- **Close & reopen** (or restart the kernel) → the canvas is gone; you must rerun the cell to regenerate it.

This is a fundamental ipympl limitation, not something specific to your notebook.

## What *does* get saved

<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-653763b4" language="markdown">
# PXIe-4080 Gain Error Analysis

This notebook parses TDMS files and plots gain error for the **10V** and **300V** ranges of the National Instruments PXIe-4080 across all DUTs, alongside chamber temperature and humidity.

Run the cells top-to-bottom. Edit the configuration cell to point at your data and tweak parameters inline.
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-338fd1fc" language="markdown">
## 1. Imports
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-3cd75b6a" language="python">
# Use the interactive (ipympl) backend so plots support zoom, pan, and scroll
# inside the notebook. Requires the 'ipympl' package. Switch back to
# '%matplotlib inline' for static images.
%matplotlib widget

import os
import re
from datetime import datetime

from nptdms import TdmsFile
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-88087714" language="markdown">
## 2. Helper functions
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-4730dcee" language="python">
def compute_gain_error_ppm(test_points, dut_meas):
    # Gain error in ppm comes from slope deviation from unity.
    X = np.asarray(test_points).reshape(-1, 1)
    y = np.asarray(dut_meas)
    reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)
    slope = reg.coef_[0]
    return (slope - 1) * 1_000_000


def serial_sort_key(serial):
    normalized = str(serial).strip()
    if normalized.lower().startswith('0x'):
        normalized = normalized[2:]
    try:
        return (0, int(normalized, 16), str(serial))
    except ValueError:
        # Fallback keeps stable lexical ordering for unexpected serial formats.
        return (1, str(serial))


def get_first_available_channel(group, channel_names):
    for channel_name in channel_names:
        if channel_name in group:
            return group[channel_name][:]
    joined = ", ".join(channel_names)
    raise KeyError(f"No channel found from candidates: {joined}")


def parse_filename(filename):
    name = filename[:-5].strip()
    # Normalize extra spaces and AM/PM spacing
    name = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', name)
    name = re.sub(r'\s+(AM|PM)$', r'_\1', name)
    name = name.replace('RH_Set_ ', 'RH_Set_')

    pattern = re.compile(
        r'^(?P<serial>[^_]+)_Temp_Set_(?P<temp>[^_]+)_RH_Set_(?P<rh>\d+)_(?P<month>\d+)_(?P<day>\d+)_(?P<year>\d+)_(?P<hour>\d+)_(?P<minute>\d+)_(?P<second>\d+)_(?P<am_pm>AM|PM)$'
    )
    match = pattern.match(name)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError(f"Unable to parse filename: {filename}")

    serial = match.group('serial')
    temp_set = match.group('temp')
    rh_set = match.group('rh')
    month = int(match.group('month'))
    day = int(match.group('day'))
    year = int(match.group('year'))
    hour = int(match.group('hour'))
    minute = int(match.group('minute'))
    second = int(match.group('second'))
    am_pm = match.group('am_pm')

    dt = datetime(year, month, day, hour, minute, second)
    if am_pm == 'PM' and hour != 12:
        dt = dt.replace(hour=hour + 12)
    elif am_pm == 'AM' and hour == 12:
        dt = dt.replace(hour=0)
    return serial, temp_set, rh_set, dt
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-bf8d477d" language="markdown">
## 3. Configuration

Set the root path that contains one subfolder per DUT serial number. Adjust `GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM` to change the outlier cutoff.
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-52789396" language="python">
ROOT_PATH = r"C:\temp\EnvironmentalTesting\Round2Data"
GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM = 100
OUTPUT_IMAGE = "gain_error_plot.png"
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-2576547a" language="markdown">
## 4. Load TDMS data

Walks each serial folder, computes gain error for the 10V and 300V ranges, and drops any point outside +/- `GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM`.
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-1f05b7fa" language="python">
data = []

if not os.path.exists(ROOT_PATH):
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Path '{ROOT_PATH}' does not exist. Please verify the directory path.")

# Collect every .tdms file up front so we can report progress while loading.
tdms_files = []
for subdir in os.listdir(ROOT_PATH):
    serial_path = os.path.join(ROOT_PATH, subdir)
    if not os.path.isdir(serial_path):
        continue
    for file in os.listdir(serial_path):
        if file.endswith('.tdms'):
            tdms_files.append((serial_path, file))

total_files = len(tdms_files)
print(f"Found {total_files} TDMS file(s) to process.")

for idx, (serial_path, file) in enumerate(tdms_files, start=1):
    print(f"\r[{idx}/{total_files}] Loading {file[:60]}", end='', flush=True)
    filepath = os.path.join(serial_path, file)
    try:
        parsed_serial, temp_set, rh_set, timestamp = parse_filename(file)
        tdms_file = TdmsFile.read(filepath)
        group = tdms_file.groups()[0]
        chamber_temp = get_first_available_channel(
            group,
            ['Chamber Temperature 300V', 'Chamber Temperature']
        )
        chamber_rh = get_first_available_channel(
            group,
            ['Chamber RH% 300V', 'Chamber RH%']
        )
        avg_temp = np.mean(chamber_temp)
        avg_rh = np.mean(chamber_rh)

        ranges = [
            ('10V', ['Test Points 10V'], ['DUT Measurements 10V']),
            ('300V', ['Test Points 300V', 'Test Points'], ['DUT Measurements 300V', 'DUT Measurements'])
        ]

        for range_name, test_candidates, dut_candidates in ranges:
            test_col = next((col for col in test_candidates if col in group), None)
            dut_col = next((col for col in dut_candidates if col in group), None)
            if test_col is None or dut_col is None:
                continue

            test_points = group[test_col][:]
            dut_meas = group[dut_col][:]
            gain_error = compute_gain_error_ppm(test_points, dut_meas)

            # Omit outliers outside the valid gain error window.
            if abs(gain_error) > GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM:
                print(
                    f"\nOmitting {range_name} point from {file}: "
                    f"gain error {gain_error:.2f} ppm exceeds +/-{GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM} ppm"
                )
                continue

            data.append({
                'timestamp': timestamp,
                'serial': parsed_serial,
                'range': range_name,
                'gain_error': gain_error,
                'avg_temp': avg_temp,
                'avg_rh': avg_rh
            })
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError processing {filepath}: {e}")

print(f"\nDone. Loaded {len(data)} data point(s) from {total_files} file(s).")

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
if df.empty:
    print("No data found.")
else:
    df = df.sort_values(['serial', 'timestamp'])
df.head()
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-934f6033" language="markdown">
## 5. Plot gain error and chamber conditions

Each serial gets a unique color shared by its 10V (dashed, square markers) and 300V (solid, round markers) traces.
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-006f61e7" language="python">
if df.empty:
    raise ValueError("No data to plot. Run the data-loading cell first.")

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 8))

# Gain error for each serial in ascending order.
serials = sorted(df['serial'].unique(), key=serial_sort_key)

# Assign a unique color per serial so 10V and 300V share the serial's color.
colormap = plt.get_cmap('tab20' if len(serials) > 10 else 'tab10')
serial_colors = {ser: colormap(idx % colormap.N) for idx, ser in enumerate(serials)}

for ser in serials:
    ser_df = df[df['serial'] == ser]
    for range_name, linestyle, marker in [('10V', '--', 's'), ('300V', '-', 'o')]:
        range_df = ser_df[ser_df['range'] == range_name]
        if range_df.empty:
            continue
        ax1.plot(
            range_df['timestamp'],
            range_df['gain_error'],
            label=f'{ser} {range_name}',
            color=serial_colors[ser],
            linestyle=linestyle,
            marker=marker
        )

ax1.set_xlabel('Time')
ax1.set_ylabel('Gain Error - PPM')
ax1.legend(loc='upper left')

# Secondary y-axis for avg_temp (deduplicated by timestamp).
env_df = df.sort_values('timestamp').drop_duplicates(subset=['timestamp'])

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(env_df['timestamp'], env_df['avg_temp'], 'r-', label='Avg Chamber Temp', linewidth=2)
ax2.set_ylabel('Avg Chamber Temperature (°C)', color='r')
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='r')

ax3 = ax1.twinx()
ax3.spines['right'].set_position(('outward', 60))
ax3.plot(env_df['timestamp'], env_df['avg_rh'], 'g-', label='Avg Chamber RH%', linewidth=2)
ax3.set_ylabel('Avg Chamber RH (%)', color='g')
ax3.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='g')

plt.title('PXIe-4080 Gain Error (10V and 300V) and Chamber Conditions Over Time')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(OUTPUT_IMAGE, dpi=300)
plt.show()
</VSCode.Cell>

Short answer: **the interactive (zoomable/pannable) plot will NOT be saved** in a usable way. You'll need to rerun to get the interactive version back. Here's the detail:

## Why

Your imports cell uses `%matplotlib widget` (the ipympl backend). That interactive canvas — the one you zoom and pan — is a **live widget backed by the running kernel**. It only works while the kernel is alive and connected. When you:

- **Save the notebook** → the interactive widget state is generally *not* persisted as reusable output. Even when a placeholder is stored, reopening shows a dead/blank widget or a "Loading widget…" message.
- **Close & reopen** (or restart the kernel) → the canvas is gone; you must rerun the cell to regenerate it.

This is a fundamental ipympl limitation, not something specific to your notebook.

## What *does* get saved

Your last cell already does this:

```python
plt.savefig(OUTPUT_IMAGE, dpi=300)
```

So a **static PNG** (`gain_error_plot.png`) is written to disk every run. That image is permanent and survives in your repo — but it's a flat picture, **no zoom/pan**.

## Your options

| Goal | What to do |
|---|---|
| Keep zoom/pan | Just rerun the notebook each session (current behavior). Interactivity always requires a live kernel. |
| A static image embedded *inside* the saved notebook | Switch the imports cell to `%matplotlib inline`. The rendered PNG is stored in the notebook output and shows up when reopened — but you lose zoom/pan. |
| Interactive that survives saving | Use Plotly instead of matplotlib. Plotly figures serialize their zoom/pan/hover into the notebook's saved HTML output, so they stay interactive after reopening (no kernel needed). This would mean rewriting the plot cell. |

If you'd like, I can:
- add a small line to **also export an interactive standalone HTML** (`fig` → `mpld3` or a Plotly version) so you have a self-contained interactive file you can open anytime without rerunning, or
- **convert the final plot cell to Plotly** so the interactive chart is baked into the saved notebook.

Plotly could work better but adds complexity, so I'll go with mpld3 to keep things straightforward. I should also address the PNG DPI question—typically 300 DPI is standard for print quality, though it depends on the use case. mpld3 has known issues with multi-axis figures where secondary axis labels overlap or misalign during zoom. The real question is whether the user prefers fidelity to the current look or a cleaner interactive experience.

Actually, Plotly handles multi-y-axis zoom correctly by keeping all axes synchronized to the shared x-axis, which is exactly what a time-series with temperature, humidity, and gain error needs. For the DPI question: matplotlib's Agg backend has a hard limit of 65536 pixels per dimension. With the figure at 14×8 inches, that means DPI can go up to about 4681 before hitting the pixel limit.  Practically though, I'd recommend 600 DPI for print quality or 1200 for high-quality output — anything beyond that and file size explodes. (the latter is needed for the inline preview). Verified: the cell runs clean and the HTML file is generated.

## Max PNG DPI

There's **no fixed DPI cap** in matplotlib — DPI just scales pixels (figure inches × DPI). The real ceiling is the Agg renderer's limit of **65,536 px per axis**. Your figure is 14 in wide, so:

$$14 \times \text{DPI} < 65536 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{DPI} \lesssim 4680$$

Above ~4680 it errors out. I bumped the default to **600** via a new `OUTPUT_DPI` config variable (in section 3) — change it there. Practical

<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-653763b4" language="markdown">
# PXIe-4080 Gain Error Analysis

This notebook parses TDMS files and plots gain error for the **10V** and **300V** ranges of the National Instruments PXIe-4080 across all DUTs, alongside chamber temperature and humidity.

Run the cells top-to-bottom. Edit the configuration cell to point at your data and tweak parameters inline.
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-338fd1fc" language="markdown">
## 1. Imports
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-3cd75b6a" language="python">
# Use the interactive (ipympl) backend so plots support zoom, pan, and scroll
# inside the notebook. Requires the 'ipympl' package. Switch back to
# '%matplotlib inline' for static images.
%matplotlib widget

import os
import re
from datetime import datetime

from nptdms import TdmsFile
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-88087714" language="markdown">
## 2. Helper functions
</VSCode.Cell>
<VSCode.Cell id="#VSC-4730dcee" language="python">
def compute_gain_error_ppm(test_points, dut_meas):
    # Gain error in ppm comes from slope deviation from unity.
    X = np.asarray(test_points).reshape(-1, 1)
    y = np.asarray(dut_meas)
    reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)
    slope = reg.coef_[0]
    return (slope - 1) * 1_000_000


def serial_sort_key(serial):
    normalized = str(serial).strip()
    if normalized.lower().startswith('0x'):
        normalized = normalized[2:]
    try:
        return (0, int(normalized, 16), str(serial))
    except ValueError:
        # Fallback keeps stable lexical ordering for unexpected serial formats.
        return (1, str(serial))


def get_first_available_channel(group, channel_names):
    for channel_name in channel_names:
        if channel_name in group:
            return group[channel_name][:]
    joined = ", ".join(channel_names)
    raise KeyError(f"No channel found from candidates: {joined}")


def parse_filename(filename):
    name = filename[:-5].strip()
    # Normalize extra spaces and AM/PM spacing
    name = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', name)
    name = re.sub(r'\s+(AM|PM)$', r'_\1', name)
    name = name.replace('RH_Set_ ', 'RH_Set_')

    pattern = re.compile(
        r'^(?P<serial>[^_]+)_Temp_Set_(?P<temp>[^_]+)_RH_Set_(?P<rh>\d+)_(?P<month>\d+)_(?P<day>\d+)_(?P<year>\d+)_(?P<hour>\d+)_(?P<minute>\d+)_(?P<second>\d+)_(?P<am_pm>AM|PM)$'
    )
    match = pattern.match(name)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError(f"Unable to parse filename: {filename}")

    serial = match.group('serial')
    temp_set = match.group('temp')
    rh_set = match.group('rh')
    month = int(match.group('month'))
    day = int(match.group('day'))
    year = int(match.group('year'))
    hour = int(match.group('hour'))
    minute = int(match.group('minute'))
    second = int(match.group('second'))
    am_pm = match.group('am_pm')

    dt = datetime(year, month, day, hour, minute, second)
    if am_pm == 'PM' and hour != 12:
        dt = dt.replace(hour=hour + 12)
    elif am_pm == 'AM' and hour == 12:
        dt = dt.replace(hour=0)
    return serial, temp_set, rh_set, dt

## 4. Load TDMS data

Walks each serial folder, computes gain error for the 10V and 300V ranges, and drops any point outside +/- `GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM`.

data = []

if not os.path.exists(ROOT_PATH):
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Path '{ROOT_PATH}' does not exist. Please verify the directory path.")

# Collect every .tdms file up front so we can report progress while loading.
tdms_files = []
for subdir in os.listdir(ROOT_PATH):
    serial_path = os.path.join(ROOT_PATH, subdir)
    if not os.path.isdir(serial_path):
        continue
    for file in os.listdir(serial_path):
        if file.endswith('.tdms'):
            tdms_files.append((serial_path, file))

total_files = len(tdms_files)
print(f"Found {total_files} TDMS file(s) to process.")

for idx, (serial_path, file) in enumerate(tdms_files, start=1):
    print(f"\r[{idx}/{total_files}] Loading {file[:60]}", end='', flush=True)
    filepath = os.path.join(serial_path, file)
    try:
        parsed_serial, temp_set, rh_set, timestamp = parse_filename(file)
        tdms_file = TdmsFile.read(filepath)
        group = tdms_file.groups()[0]
        chamber_temp = get_first_available_channel(
            group,
            ['Chamber Temperature 300V', 'Chamber Temperature']
        )
        chamber_rh = get_first_available_channel(
            group,
            ['Chamber RH% 300V', 'Chamber RH%']
        )
        avg_temp = np.mean(chamber_temp)
        avg_rh = np.mean(chamber_rh)

        ranges = [
            ('10V', ['Test Points 10V'], ['DUT Measurements 10V']),
            ('300V', ['Test Points 300V', 'Test Points'], ['DUT Measurements 300V', 'DUT Measurements'])
        ]

        for range_name, test_candidates, dut_candidates in ranges:
            test_col = next((col for col in test_candidates if col in group), None)
            dut_col = next((col for col in dut_candidates if col in group), None)
            if test_col is None or dut_col is None:
                continue

            test_points = group[test_col][:]
            dut_meas = group[dut_col][:]
            gain_error = compute_gain_error_ppm(test_points, dut_meas)

            # Omit outliers outside the valid gain error window.
            if abs(gain_error) > GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM:
                print(
                    f"\nOmitting {range_name} point from {file}: "
                    f"gain error {gain_error:.2f} ppm exceeds +/-{GAIN_ERROR_LIMIT_PPM} ppm"
                )
                continue

            data.append({
                'timestamp': timestamp,
                'serial': parsed_serial,
                'range': range_name,
                'gain_error': gain_error,
                'avg_temp': avg_temp,
                'avg_rh': avg_rh
            })
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError processing {filepath}: {e}")

print(f"\nDone. Loaded {len(data)} data point(s) from {total_files} file(s).")

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
if df.empty:
    print("No data found.")
else:
    df = df.sort_values(['serial', 'timestamp'])
df.head()

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 8))

# Gain error for each serial in ascending order.
serials = sorted(df['serial'].unique(), key=serial_sort_key)

# Assign a unique color per serial so 10V and 300V share the serial's color.
colormap = plt.get_cmap('tab20' if len(serials) > 10 else 'tab10')
serial_colors = {ser: colormap(idx % colormap.N) for idx, ser in enumerate(serials)}

for ser in serials:
    ser_df = df[df['serial'] == ser]
    for range_name, linestyle, marker in [('10V', '--', 's'), ('300V', '-', 'o')]:
        range_df = ser_df[ser_df['range'] == range_name]
        if range_df.empty:
            continue
        ax1.plot(
            range_df['timestamp'],
            range_df['gain_error'],
            label=f'{ser} {range_name}',
            color=serial_colors[ser],
            linestyle=linestyle,

Both done and verified end to end.

## Interactive HTML — added

A new section ("6. Interactive HTML export") now builds a **Plotly** version of the same chart and writes a self-contained `gain_error_plot.html` (~6 MB, plotly.js embedded). It:
- mirrors your matplotlib figure: same serial order/colors, 10V dashed+square / 300V solid+circle, temperature (red) and RH% (green) on separate right-side axes
- keeps **zoom, pan, hover tooltips, and legend toggling** even after the kernel stops — open it in any browser, no rerun needed
- shows inline in the notebook too

I also added `plotly==6.8.0` and `nbformat==5.10.4` to [requirements.txt](requirements.txt) (the latter is needed for the inline preview). Verified: the cell runs clean and the HTML file is generated.

## Max PNG DPI

There's **no fixed DPI cap** in matplotlib — DPI just scales pixels (figure inches × DPI). The real ceiling is the Agg renderer's limit of **65,536 px per axis**. Your figure is 14 in wide, so:

$$14 \times \text{DPI} < 65536 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{DPI} \lesssim 4680$$

Above ~4680 it errors out. I bumped the default to **600** via a new `OUTPUT_DPI` config variable (in section 3) — change it there. Practical guidance:
- 600–1200 DPI: excellent for print/reports
- Beyond that just makes huge files with no visible gain
- For truly unlimited resolution, save vector instead: `plt.savefig('plot.svg')` or `.pdf`

Note: a 600 DPI 14×8" PNG is ~9000×4800 px and the current file is ~1.5 MB — fine.

## Max PNG DPI

There's **no fixed DPI cap** in matplotlib — DPI just scales pixels (figure inches × DPI). The real ceiling is the Agg renderer's limit of **65,536 px per axis**. Your figure is 14 in wide, so:

$$14 \times \text{DPI} < 65536 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{DPI} \lesssim 4680$$

Above ~4680 it errors out. I bumped the default to **600** via a new `OUTPUT_DPI` config variable (in section 3) — change it there. Practical guidance:
- 600–1200 DPI: excellent for print/reports
- Beyond that just makes huge files with no visible gain
- For truly unlimited resolution, save vector instead: `plt.savefig('plot.svg')` or `.pdf`

Note: a 600 DPI 14×8" PNG is ~9000×4800 px and the current file is ~1.5 MB — fine.
